article_title: Warwick's RAVEN AI Pipeline Validates 118 New Exoplanets in TESS Data, Adding Nearly 1,000 Fresh Candidates

content_preview.title: Warwick's RAVEN AI Pipeline Validates 118 New Exoplanets in TESS Data, Adding Nearly 1,000 Fresh Candidates
content_preview.summary: A University of Warwick team applied a new machine-learning pipeline called RAVEN to four years of TESS observations, statistically validating 118 short-period planets and surfacing roughly 1,000 candidates not previously catalogued.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers at the University of Warwick have used a new AI pipeline called RAVEN to comb through more than 2.2 million stars observed during the first four years of NASA's TESS mission, statistically validating 118 new transiting planets and flagging nearly 1,000 high-quality candidates that had not previously been catalogued, [according to a Warwick press release](https://warwick.ac.uk/news/pressreleases/ai-approach-uncovers-dozens-of-hidden-planets/). The work is published in the...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: High-quality 764-word News piece on the Warwick RAVEN paper. Structure (Overview / What We Know / Methodology / Why It Matters / What We Don't Know) is clean. Quantitative claims are precise (2.2M stars, 16-day cutoff, 118 validated, ~1,000 newly catalogued, 0.08% Neptunian desert, 9-10% Sun-like close-in planet rate, 10x Kepler precision). Tone neutral; the closing 'What We Don't Know' section properly bounds the result (statistical validation vs. radial-velocity confirmation, short-period regime only).
editor_notes.source_verification:
All 6 sources verified — 5 from snapshots (Phys.org came via Archive.org fallback automatically), 1 via WebFetch fallback (academic.oup.com returned HTTP 403).

• source-0.html.gz (Warwick press release): Confirms 118 new planets + over 2,000 candidates + nearly 1,000 entirely new, attributed to Marina Lafarga Magro (verbatim quote); RAVEN expansion 'RAnking and Validation of ExoplaNets' (also visible in references); 2.2M stars, 16-day orbital cutoff; 0.08% Neptunian desert figure; verbatim Hadjigeorghiou quote 'In addition, RAVEN is designed to handle the whole process in one go, from detecting the signal, to vetting it with machine learning and statistically validating it'; verbatim Hadjigeorghiou 'The challenge lies in identifying...'; verbatim Cui 'For the first time, we can put a precise number on just how empty this 'desert' is'; verbatim Armstrong 'RAVEN allows us to analyse enormous datasets...'; David Armstrong as Associate Professor and senior co-author.

• source-1 (academic.oup.com / MNRAS, WebFetch fallback after HTTP 403): Confirms paper title verbatim 'Automatic search for transiting planets in TESS–SPOC FFIs with raven: over 100 newly validated planets and over 2000 vetted candidates'; volume 548, issue 3 (May 2026); full author list 'M Lafarga, D J Armstrong, K Cui, A Hadjigeorghiou, V Kunovac, L Doyle, E M Bryant, R F Díaz, L A Nieto, A Osborn' verbatim.

• source-2.html.gz (Space.com): Confirms verbatim 'spots exoplanets by recording the tiny dips in starlight they cause when they pass in front of the face of the parent star'.

• source-3.html.gz (Phys.org via Archive.org): Confirms the full RAVEN expansion 'RAVEN: RAnking and Validation of ExoplaNets', the Lafarga 118+2,000+1,000 quote, and the broader piece content.

• source-4.html.gz (EurekAlert): Confirms 9-10% close-in planet rate around Sun-like stars; Kepler comparison; 'uncertainties up to ten times smaller'; 0.08% Neptunian desert.

• source-5.html.gz (ScienceDaily): Confirms ultra-short-period planets 'in less than a day' / 'in under 24 hours' framing; Neptunian desert classification.

Every inline link points to a source containing the specific claim. No misattribution. No fabricated quantitative claims.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All factual claims verified verbatim. Job titles correctly distinguished (Lafarga as first author / postdoc; Hadjigeorghiou as pipeline development lead; Cui as population/desert-study lead; Armstrong as senior co-author / Associate Professor). 9-10% / 0.08% / 16-day / 2.2M numbers all match. The MNRAS paper citation (volume 548, issue 3) verified.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, professional, technical. The headline figure (118 validated, ~1,000 fresh candidates) is well-bounded against the statistical nature of validation in 'What We Don't Know'. No sensationalism.
editor_notes.originality: Searched archive for 'TESS', 'exoplanet', 'RAVEN', 'Warwick' — no prior coverage of this specific paper. Topic is genuinely new.
recommendations: Future housekeeping: academic.oup.com (Oxford University Press journals — primary source for many MNRAS, OUP-published peer-reviewed papers) is a strong allowlist candidate.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. High-quality scientific reporting with all claims verified across 6 sources. Verdict driven by academic.oup.com allowlist gap and HTTP 403 (resolved via WebFetch). Article is publishable as-is.
